findings.reachability: The function is called internally whenever a language switcher renders flag images; no direct user trigger required, but the input ($flag_url) originates from Polylang's own stored flag URLs.
findings.justification: The regex /polylang\/flags\/(\w+).(?:jpg|png|svg)$/i has an unescaped dot before the extension alternation group, so it matches any character in that position (e.g. 'en_jpg' or 'enXpng') instead of only a literal dot — this is a correctness flaw but has negligible security impact because (a) the captured group is still restricted to \w+ and (b) the URL source is Polylang's own internal data.

detection_pattern.section_name: Regex: unescaped dot before extension alternation
detection_pattern.description: Catches regex literals where a dot immediately preceding a non-capturing extension group (?:jpg|png|svg) is not backslash-escaped, making it a wildcard instead of a literal dot; the anti-pattern checks for the properly escaped version.

findings.reachability: cpel_flag_svg() is a public PHP function; all internal callers pass $flag_code pre-filtered through cpel_flag_code() (\w+ restriction), but external/third-party code calling the function directly with unsanitized input could pass path-traversal sequences.
findings.justification: The function builds $flag_path = "/assets/flags/$flag_code.svg" and passes CPEL_DIR . $flag_path to file_exists() without validating $flag_code internally; a caller supplying '../../../wp-config' would cause file_exists() to check an arbitrary path outside the plugin directory — limited to existence oracle, not read/execute, and current callers are safe, but no defense-in-depth guard exists inside the function.

detection_pattern.section_name: Path traversal: unvalidated variable in filesystem path
detection_pattern.description: Detects file_exists() (or similar filesystem calls) where a plugin constant is concatenated with an unsanitized variable; the anti-pattern checks for sanitization functions applied to the variable before the filesystem call.

findings.reachability: Triggered every time cpel_flag_emoji() is called, which happens on every Elementor editor panel render for each language; PHP opcode cache mitigates performance but the redundant include is a code-quality issue.
findings.justification: The emojis.php file is included on every invocation of cpel_flag_emoji() rather than being cached or loaded once; __DIR__ is hardcoded so there is no LFI risk, but the pattern of include inside a frequently-called function is an unintentional resource amplification that can be exploited if the include path were ever made configurable.

detection_pattern.section_name: Resource amplification: file include inside function body without static cache
detection_pattern.description: Catches PHP functions that contain a bare include/require statement without a static variable guard; each call re-reads and re-evaluates the file. Anti-pattern checks for include_once or static caching of the result.
